title: Galerkin-finite difference method for fractional parabolic partial differential equations
author: Hossan, Datta, et al.

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: We solve the Eq. (3) with the help of the Galerkin Weighted Residual method. In this method, basis functions need to meet the homogeneous boundary requirements. And initial condition is another important step in solving any kind of partial differential equation. In this case, we select initially a trial solution. Let us consider such an approximate solution of the Eq. (3) as(5)u˜(x,τ)=Φ0(x,τ)+∑j=1naj(τ)Φj(x) where Φ0(x,τ) satisfies the non-homogeneous part of the boundary conditions, Φj(x) denotes the basis functions which must fulfill the homogeneous boundary requirements, and the unknown parameters, aj(τ) are function of time [44].

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: Now the residual function is(6)R(x,τ)=∂u˜∂τ−q(x)∂βu˜∂xβ−s(x,τ)Substituting the Eq. (5) into (6) we get,(7)∂u˜∂τ=∑j=1ndaj(τ)dτΦj(x)+∂Φ0(x,τ)∂τand(8)∂βu˜∂xβ=∑j=1naj(τ)dβΦj(x)dxβ+∂βΦ0(x,τ)∂xβThen the weighted residual equation is(9)∫01R(x,τ)Φi(x)dx=0where, Φi(x) are the basis functions defined in (2) for all i=1,2,...,n.

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: Substituting (7) and (8) into Eq. (6), then (9) leads us,(10)∫01(∑j=1ndaj(τ)dτΦj(x)+∂Φ0(x,τ)∂τ−q(x)(∑j=1naj(τ)dβΦj(x)dxβ+∂βΦ0(x,τ)∂xβ)−s(x,τ))Φi(x)dx=0

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: Now(11)∑j=1ndaj(τ)dτ∫01Φj(x)Φi(x)dx+∫01∂Φ0(x,τ)∂τΦi(x)dx−∑j=1naj(τ)∫01(dβΦj(x)dxβ)Φi(x)q(x)dx−∫01(∂βϕ0(x,τ)∂xβ)Φi(x)q(x)dx−∫01s(x,τ)Φi(x)dx=0 which can be rewritten as(12)∑j=1ndaj(τ)dτ∫01Φj(x)Φi(x)dx−∑j=1naj(τ)∫01(dβΦj(x)dxβ)Φi(x)q(x)dx=∫01(∂βΦ0(x,τ)∂xβ)Φi(x)q(x)dx−∫01∂Φ0(x,τ)∂τΦi(x)dx+∫01s(x,τ)Φi(x)dxWe can define the integrals of the above equation in terms of matrix. The dimension of the matrix depends on the number of parameters or basis functions. In matrix notation, the Eq. (12) reduces to(13)[C]{da(τ)dτ}+[K]{a(τ)}={F}where,Cij=∫01Φj(x)Φi(x)dxKij=−∫01(∂β∂xβΦj(x))Φi(x)q(x)dxFi=∫01(∂βΦ0(x,τ)∂xβq(x)−∂Φ0(x,τ)∂τ+s(x,τ))Φi(x)dx

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: This method is a combination of discretization of time fractional terms [34], and the modified Galerkin method for space fractional terms. Now we are ready to provide the mathematical formulation for the Eq. (4) explicitly. For this, we introduce the discretized approximation of the time-fractional derivative [34] as:∂αu(x,τn+1)∂τα=(δτ)−αΓ(2−α){u(x,τn+1)−u(x,τn)+∑l=1n(u(x,τn+1−l)−u(x,τn−l))((l+1)(1−α)−l(1−α))} which is equivalent to,(14)∂αu(x,τn+1)∂τα=V[u(x,τn+1)−u(x,τn)+∑l=1n(u(x,τn+1−l)−u(x,τn−l))Pl]where, Pl=((l+1)(1−α)−l(1−α)), V=(δτ)−αΓ(2−α) and time step size as, δτ=τN

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: After applying Galerkin method, we calculate u(x,τn) at different values of x, and at any specified time step, τn which is defined for n=1,2,...,N.

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: We choose the approximate solution as,(15)u˜(x,τn)=Φ0(x,τn)+∑i=1kai(τn)Φi(x)where, j represents the number of parameters. Unlike as Galerkin method, Φ0(x,τn) calculates the non-homogeneous part of the boundary conditions at any specified value of time, τn,ai(τn) are the unknown parameters whose values are needed to be determined again at the specified time, τn and Φi(x) are basis functions such as we have discussed in (2). Now we define the residual function asR(x,τn+1)=V[u˜(x,τn+1)−u˜(x,τn)+∑l=1n(u˜(x,τn+1−l)−u˜(x,τn−l))Pl]−q(x)[∂βu˜(x,τn+1)∂xβ]−s(x,τn+1)To find a set of linear equations, we consider:(16)∫01R(x,τn+1)Φj(x)dx=0wherej=1,2,...,kor equivalently,(17)∫01(V[u˜(x,τn+1)−u˜(x,τn)+∑l=1n(u˜(x,τn+1−l)−u˜(x,τn−l))Pl]−q(x)[∂βu˜(x,τn+1)∂xβ]−s(x,τn+1))Φj(x)dx=0

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: After substitution of u˜ from Eq. (15) into Eq. (17) we get,∫01(V[(Φ0(x,τn+1)+∑i=1kai(τn+1)Φi(x))−(Φ0(x,τn)+∑i=1kai(τn)Φi(x))+∑l=1n((Φ0(x,τn+1−l)+∑i=1kai(τn+1−l)Φi(x))−(Φ0(x,τn−l)+∑i=1kai(τn−l)Φi(x)))Pl]−q(x)[∂β(Φ0(x,τn+1)+∑i=1kai(τn+1)Φi(x))∂xβ]−s(x,τn+1))Φj(x)dx=0or,V[∫01((Φ0(x,τn+1)+∑i=1kai(τn+1)Φi(x))Φj(x))dx−∫01((Φ0(x,τn)+∑i=1kai(τn)Φi(x))Φj(x))dx+∑l=1n(∫01((Φ0(x,τn+1−l)+∑i=1kai(τn+1−l)Φi(x))Φj(x))dx−∫01((Φ0(x,τn−l)+∑i=1kai(τn−l)Φi(x))Φj(x))dx)Pl]−∫01q(x)[∂β(Φ0(x,τn+1)∂xβ+∑i=1kai(τn+1)∂β(Φi(x))∂xβ]Φj(x)dx−∫01s(x,τn+1)Φj(x)dx=0 or,(18)V[∫01((Φ0(x,τn+1)−Φ0(x,τn))Φj(x)dx+∑i=1k(ai(τn+1)−ai(τn))∫01Φi(x)Φj(x)dx+∑l=1n(∫01((Φ0(x,τn+1−l)−Φ0(x,τn−l))Φj(x)dx+∑i=1k(ai(τn+1−l)−ai(τn−l))×∫01Φi(x)Φj(x)dx)Pl]−∫01q(x)(∂β(Φ0(x,τn+1))∂xβ)Φj(x)dx−∑i=1kai(τn+1)×∫01q(x)∂β(Φi(x))∂xβΦj(x)dx−∫01s(x,τn+1)Φj(x)dx=0

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: We evaluate the values of unknown parameters, ai(τn) after substituting the value of n in Eq. (18). For example, if we substitute n=0, we obtain linear equations at τ=δτ. Solving those equations we find out an approximate solution at a specified time value as a function of x such as u˜(x,δτ). Repeating the steps for n=1,2,...,N, we will be able to calculate the values of u(x,τ) at different x for any value of time. We express the integrals in terms of matrix and in matrix notation,(19)V[[G]{a(τn+1)−a(τn)}+∑l=1n({H}+[G]{a(τn+1−l)−a(τn−l)})Pl]=[K]{a(τn+1)}+{M}whereGij=∫01Φi(x)Φj(x)dxHj=∫01((Φ0(x,τn+1−l)−Φ0(x,τn−l))Φj(x)dxKij=∫01q(x)(∂β∂xβΦi(x))Φj(x)dxMj=∫01(q(x)∂β(Φ0(x,τn+1))∂xβ−V(Φ0(x,τn+1)−Φ0(x,τn))+s(x,τn+1))Φj(x)dx

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: To simplify the convergence analysis of the Galerkin-Finite Difference method, we consider the following fractional diffusion equation in both space and time but without a source term and a constant diffusion coefficient (K).(20)∂αu∂τα=K∂βu∂xβIf we write the residual function setting to zero we get,(21)V[u˜(x,τn+1)−u˜(x,τn)+∑i=1n(u˜(x,τn+1−i)−u˜(x,τn−i))pi]=K[∂βu˜(x,τn+1)∂xβ]Particularly, for n=0, (τ1=δt) the Eq. (21) can be written as,(22)u˜(x,τ1)−KV[∂βu˜(x,τ1)∂xβ]=p0u˜(x,τ0)and, in general, we get the following equation,(23)u˜(x,τn+1)−KV[∂βu˜(x,τn+1)∂xβ]=(1−p1)u˜(x,τn)+pnu˜(x,τ0)+∑i=1n−1(pi−pi+1)u˜(x,τn−i)

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Now, through letting ∥ϵ1∥∞=|ϵj1|where,xj=max(xm), we can write,|ϵj1|≤|ϵj1|−KV[Dβ(|ϵj1|)]≤|ϵj1−KV[Dβ(ϵj1)]|=|ϵj0+Rj1|Since, ϵ0=0and,|Rj1|≤C(δτ)then,∥ϵ1∥∞≤C(δτ) For the next part of the proof we consider the following:∥ϵi∥∞≤Cpi−1−1(δτ),pi−1≥pn−1and∥ϵn+1∥∞=|ϵjn+1|;where,xj=max(xm).Now,|ϵjn+1|≤|ϵjn+1|−KV[Dβ(|ϵjn+1|)]≤|ϵjn+1−KV[Dβ(ϵjn+1)]|=|(1−p1)ϵjn+pnϵj0+∑i=1n−1(pi−pi+1)ϵjn−i+Rjn+1|≤(1−p1)|ϵjn|+∑i=1n−1(pi−pi+1)|ϵjn−i|+|Rjn+1|≤{(1−p1)pn−1−1+∑i=1n−1(pi−pi+1)pn−i−1−1}C(δτ)+|Rjn+1|So, ∥ϵn+1∥∞≤pn−1{(1−p1)+∑i=1n−1(pi−pi+1)+pn}C(δτ)=pn−1C(δτ). Therefore, the convergence order of the scheme is given by|u˜mn−u(xm,τn)|≤C(δτ)where,n=1,2,...,Nandm=1,2,...,M−1.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Throughout this, we have exploited C as a positive constant with (0.5<C<2) depending on the problem. The following theorem is derived for the case, 0<α<1. Based on the above discussion and literature [[34], [45]], we can state the following similar theorem:

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Theorem: Consider the approximate solution as u˜(xm,τn) of the exact solution u(xm,τn), and for simplification, we choose, u˜(xm,τn)=u˜mn and u(xm,τn)=umn. Then there is always a positive constant C such that,|u˜mn−umn|≤C(δτ)where,n=1,2,...,Nandm=1,2,...,M−1.

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: For stability analysis, we insert (14) into (20) to get(24)V[u(x,τn+1)−u(x,τn)+∑i=1n(u(x,τn+1−i)−u(x,τn−i))pi]=K[∂βu(x,τn+1)∂xβ]On using (15), (21) and (24) we can write(25)V[u(x,τn+1)−u˜(x,τn+1)−(u(x,τn)−u˜(x,τn))+∑i=1n(u(x,τn+1−i)−u˜(x,τn+1−i)−(u(x,τn−i)−u˜(x,τn−i)))pi]=K[∂βu(x,τn+1)∂xβ−∂βu˜(x,τn+1)∂xβ]

steps.instruction: reduces to(29)V[Em2−Em1+(Em1−Em0)p1)]=K∂β∂xβ(Em2).Using the notation ∥E2∥∞=|Ej2|, we obtain.(30)∥E2∥∞=|Ej2|≤|Ej2|−KV(∂β∂xβ|Ej2|)=|(1−p1)Ej1+p1Ej0|≤(1−p1)|Ej0|+p1|Ej0|≤∥E0∥∞Similarly, by using method of induction we can show that for any l-th time step,(31)∥El+1∥∞≤∥E0∥∞Thus, we may conclude that the finite difference of the time fractional term is unconditionally stable, and details are available in [34].

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Now, we consider four problems, including two applications that appear in Financial Mathematics. Here we define an absolute error, L∞-norm and order of convergence, respectively, as follows,AbsoluteError=|u˜(x,t)−u(x,t)|L∞-norm=max(Ai),whereAiaretheabsoluteerrors.OrderofConvergence,C(δt)=e1e2where, e1 and e2 represents L∞- norm for different time step size.

(3) and is taken from [32], on a specified domain, 0 0and,u(x,0)=x3.The exact solution is:u(x,t)=x2(1−x)e−tUsing the mathematical formulation described for space fractional PDE with two parameters let us approximate the solution in the form:(33)u˜(x,t)=∑i=12ai(t)Φi(x).We obtain the following residual function:(34)R(x,t)=∂u˜(x,t)∂t−(q(x)∂1.8u˜(x,t)∂x1.8+s(x,t))and the residual equation force us(35)∫01R(x,t)Φj(x)dx=0where,j=1,2or,∑i=12dai(t)dt∫01Φi(x)Φj(x)dx−∑i=12ai(t)∫01(d1.8dx1.8Φi(x))Φj(x)q(x)dx−∫01s(x,t)Φj(x)dx=0In matrix notation, we define,(36)[C]{da(τ)dτ}+[K]{a(τ)}={F}where,Cij=∫01Φj(x)Φi(x)dxKij=−∫01(∂1.8∂x1.8Φj(x))Φi(x)q(x)dxFi=∫01s(x,τ)Φi(x)dx

steps.instruction: Here, Φ1(x)=x2−x, Φ2(x)=x2−3x22+x3. Hence, we obtain,

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: In [46], shifted Chebyshev polynomials of the second kind were used to solve the problem. Abbas and Mehdi solved the space fractional diffusion equation using a numerical technique based on the shifted Legendre tau method in [30]. In [31], the fractional diffusion equation is reduced to a system of ordinary differential equations by using the properties of Chebyshev polynomials. In [32], the collocation method using the fourth kind of Chebyshev polynomial and compact finite difference method has been used. Now we compute approximate values and absolute errors using two parameters which are displayed in Table 1, Table 2, and Table 3 at different values of time. In these tables, we compare our obtained results with the existing values [30], [31], [32], [46]. These tables show that the Galerkin method provides us with better accuracy for various values of x, and we may observe that the Galerkin approach has outperformed the existing methods described in [30], [31], [32], [46]. The present view of Fig. 1 shows the absolute errors of our proposed method for space fractional PDE.Table 1Absolute errors for Problem-1 at t=1.Table 1xwith N=3 in [46]with N=3 in [32]with N=7 in [31] reported in [32], [46]Present method with N=20.105.46×10−063.17×10−094.26×10−53.52×10−150.208.51×10−065.85×10−095.39×10−51.13×10−140.309.60×10−067.97×10−096.12×10−52.14×10−140.409.18×10−069.44×10−096.48×10−53.19×10−140.507.69×10−061.02×10−086.45×10−54.11×10−140.605.60×10−061.01×10−085.98×10−54.60×10−140.703.33×10−069.12×10−095.23×10−54.77×10−140.801.34×10−067.17×10−094.48×10−54.13×10−140.908.39×10−084.16×10−093.91×10−52.61×10−14Table 2Absolute errors for Problem-1 at t=2.Table 2xwith N=3 in [46]with N=5 in [30]with N=3 in [31]with N=7 in [32]Present method with N=20.103.33×10−064.47×10−064.18×10−061.28×10−085.71×10−150.205.65×10−062.78×10−075.45×10−062.05×10−081.13×10−140.307.05×10−065.81×10−066.18×10−062.40×10−081.62×10−140.407.64×10−061.02×10−056.49×10−062.40×10−082.02×10−140.507.52×10−061.17×10−056.40×10−062.15×10−082.28×10−140.606.80×10−061.08×10−055.95×10−061.72×10−082.35×10−140.705.59×10−068.54×10−065.32×10−061.21×10−082.20×10−140.803.98×10−066.06×10−064.60×10−066.93×10−091.79×10−140.902.08×10−063.67×10−063.79×10−062.62×10−091.07×10−14Table 3Absolute errors for Problem-1 at t=10.Table 3xwith N=7 in [32]with N=7 in [46]Present method with N=20.202.28×10−092.34×10−088.88×10−180.404.21×10−094.78×10−091.28×10−170.601.15×10−097.39×10−091.24×10−170.802.71×10−102.84×10−087.92×10−18Fig. 1Absolute errors for Problem-1.Fig. 1

steps.instruction: we get a set of linear equations which provide us the values of unknowns. Repeating the process for increasing time steps we obtain our desired result. For this problem, we take the basis functions as:

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: The solution of this problem is not similar to Eq. (37) due to the discretization of the time-fractional derivative. So, our approximated solution is represented at t=1 as, u(x,1)=1.01x3−1.02x2+0.0127x and at t=0.5 as, u(x,0.5)=0.219x3−0.222x2+0.00323x

steps.title: Method validation
steps.instruction: Now, we compute absolute error and order of convergence to compare the results with the existing literature [47], which are shown in Tables 4 and 5 for different values of α and β.Table 4Error analysis for Problem-2 for α=0.4 and β=1.75.Table 4δtFor δx=11000 in [45]Present method for δx=110Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(δt)Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(δt)1208.535166×10−05-4.521834×10−03-1402.901776×10−051.55653.495112×10−031.29381809.718379×10−061.57812.553417×10−031.368811603.176376×10−061.61331.804433×10−031.4151Table 5Error analysis for Problem-2 for α=0.5 and β=1.88.Table 5δtFor δx=11000 in [45]Present method for δx=110Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(δt)Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(δt)1201.326761×10−04-5.600296×10−03-1404.803713×10−051.46574.587591×10−031.22071801.722465×10−051.47973.550801×10−031.291911606.114506×10−061.49422.660682×10−031.3345

steps.title: We generate Table 7 and Table...
steps.instruction: We generate Table 7 and Table 8 by changing one of the parameters (α, β) and keeping fixed the other. According to the tables, for fixed β=1.6 we get better values of u(x,t) at α=0.

steps.title: and for fixed α=0
steps.instruction: and for fixed α=0.5 we get good approximation values of u(x,t) at β=1.

description: We provide the first Bernoulli polynomials and their modified versions to use as basis functions for the weighted residual methods throughout this paper.
